Hewlett Packard Enterprise Data Science Institute

Power Automate + osTicket Integration Guide

Microsoft Forms → Power Automate → osTicket XML Ticket Pipeline
HPE Data Science Institute — Research Computing Data Core Version 1.1 Last Updated: March 31, 2026
Critical — Service Account Only

All Power Automate flows must be created and managed from the authorized DSI department service account (e.g., dsiserv@CougarNet.UH.EDU). Never create flows from your personal UH account. If you do not have access, contact your supervisor before proceeding.

Critical — Mandatory XML Keys

The osTicket system rejects tickets if these XML keys are missing or malformed:

XML Key Must Contain Notes
<name> Full name Combine First + Last Name from form responses
<email> Responder email Use body/responder (auto-populated by MS Forms)
<cougarnet> CougarNet username Mandatory. Must map to the CougarNet field in the form
<cluster> Cluster name Mandatory for cluster forms. The value comes from the Microsoft Form (the form question gives the user a choice of Sabine_ or Carya_). These are the only two values the ticketing system accepts.
Important — Cluster Values Come from the Microsoft Form

The <cluster> value is not something you type manually in Power Automate. It is pulled dynamically from the Microsoft Form response. The form question presents the user with two choices: Sabine_ and Carya_ (with trailing underscores). When building the XML, you map this field just like any other: @{outputs('Get_response_details')?['body/FIELD_ID_CLUSTER']}. If the form itself doesn’t have these exact values configured, the ticket will be rejected.

Table of Contents

  1. Overview & Architecture
  2. Prerequisites
  3. Create Automated Cloud Flow
  4. Name Flow & Select Trigger

1 Overview & Architecture

This guide walks you through connecting a Microsoft Form to the DSI osTicket system via Power Automate. When someone submits a form, Power Automate captures the response, formats it as an XML ticket, and sends it to osTicket via an HTTP POST request.

Microsoft Form Submission  →  Power Automate Trigger  →  Get Response Details  →  HTTP POST (XML)  →  osTicket

Every form gets its own dedicated Power Automate flow. The HTML ticket body lives inside a <![CDATA[]]> section, which is the only part you customize per form. The outer XML envelope (<ticket>, <name>, <email>, <cougarnet>, <cluster>) stays structurally the same.

2 Prerequisites

  • Access to the DSI department service account on Power Automate — never use a personal account
  • The Microsoft Form must already be created and published under the DSI service account
  • The osTicket API endpoint URL — currently https://ost-proxy.times.uh.edu/api/tickets.xml
  • The X-API-Key for osTicket (ask your supervisor if you don’t have it)
  • Basic familiarity with Power Automate’s visual flow editor

3 Step-by-Step Flow Creation

1

Create an Automated Cloud Flow

Log into make.powerautomate.com using the DSI service account. Click “+ Create” in the left sidebar, then select “Automated cloud flow”.

Figure 1 — Power Automate home. Select “Automated cloud flow.”
Click image to enlarge
2

Name the Flow & Select the Trigger

In the dialog:

  • Flow name: Use the exact form name (e.g., MICRO-CREDENTIAL IN ADVANCED DATA SCIENCE – APPLICATION FORM).
  • Trigger: Select “When a new response is submitted” from Microsoft Forms.

Click Create.

Figure 2 — Name the flow and select the Microsoft Forms trigger.
Click image to enlarge
3

Choose Your Form

Click the trigger block. In the Parameters panel, open the Form Id dropdown and select the form you’re creating the flow for.

Figure 3 — Form Id dropdown shows all forms under the service account.
Click image to enlarge
Tip

If the form doesn’t appear in the dropdown, it was likely created under a different account. Forms must be owned by the same service account running the flow.

4

Add “Get Response Details” Action

Click the + button below the trigger. Search for “Get response details” and select it from the Microsoft Forms connector.

Figure 4 — Search “Get response details” under Microsoft Forms.
Click image to enlarge
5

Configure Response Details

In the action parameters:

  • Form Id: Select the same form from Step 3.
  • Response Id: Click the field and select “Response Id” from the dynamic content panel (this comes from the trigger).
Figure 5 — Form Id selected and Response Id mapped from the trigger.
Click image to enlarge
6

Add the HTTP Action

Click + again to add another action. In the “Add an action” panel, look under Built-in tools and select HTTP.

Figure 6 — Find HTTP under Built-in tools.
Click image to enlarge
7

Configure the HTTP Action

This is the core step where you configure the HTTP POST request to osTicket. Fill in each field as follows:

Field Value
URI https://ost-proxy.times.uh.edu/api/tickets.xml
Method POST
Headers — Key X-API-Key
Headers — Value Your osTicket API key (get this from your supervisor — do not hardcode it in shared docs)
Body The full XML ticket template (see Section 5 below). Paste the entire XML code here.
Figure 7 — HTTP action fully configured: URI, POST method, X-API-Key header, and the XML body with dynamic content fields from Microsoft Forms.
Click image to enlarge
Important

In the Body field, you’ll see that Power Automate renders dynamic content (like First Name, Last Name, Cougarnet ID, etc.) as blue pill-shaped tags. These correspond to the @{outputs('Get_response_details')?['body/...']} expressions in the XML. You can insert them by clicking in the Body field and choosing fields from the dynamic content panel on the right.

Note

The complete three-step flow should now look like this on the canvas: When a new response is submitted → Get response details → HTTP. Save the flow after configuration.

4 XML Template Structure

Below is the general template you paste into the HTTP Body field. Replace placeholder IDs (e.g., FIELD_ID_FIRSTNAME) with actual response field IDs from your form.

<!-- Paste this into the HTTP Body field -->
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<ticket alert="true" autorespond="true" source="API">

    <!-- MANDATORY: Full name -->
    <name>@{outputs('Get_response_details')?['body/FIELD_ID_FIRSTNAME']}
          @{outputs('Get_response_details')?['body/FIELD_ID_LASTNAME']}</name>

    <!-- MANDATORY: Responder email -->
    <email>@{outputs('Get_response_details')?['body/responder']}</email>

    <!-- Ticket subject line -->
    <subject>Your Form Title Here</subject>

    <phone></phone>

    <!-- MANDATORY: CougarNet ID -->
    <cougarnet>@{outputs('Get_response_details')?['body/FIELD_ID_COUGARNET']}</cougarnet>

    <!-- MANDATORY for cluster forms: Value comes FROM the MS Form -->
    <!-- The form gives users a choice of Sabine_ or Carya_       -->
    <!-- Leave empty for non-cluster forms                        -->
    <cluster>@{outputs('Get_response_details')?['body/FIELD_ID_CLUSTER']}</cluster>

    <message type="text/html"><![CDATA[
    <!-- ============================================= -->
    <!--  ONLY EDIT INSIDE THIS CDATA BLOCK              -->
    <!--  This is the HTML table shown in the ticket     -->
    <!-- ============================================= -->

    <h3>YOUR FORM TITLE</h3>
    <table width="800" border="1" cellpadding="10" cellspacing="0">

    <!-- Section Header (gray row) -->
    <tr bgcolor="#e8e8e8">
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Section Name</strong></td>
    </tr>

    <!-- Data Row -->
    <tr>
      <td width="280"><strong>Field Label:</strong></td>
      <td>@{outputs('Get_response_details')?['body/FIELD_ID']}</td>
    </tr>

    <!-- Add more rows as needed -->

    </table>
    ]]></message>

</ticket>
Column Width

The left column width="280" is set on the first data row only. This controls the width for the entire column. Increase to 300 for forms with very long labels, or reduce to 200 for simpler forms.

Escaping Ampersands

If a field label contains & (like “Excel & PowerBI”), you must write it as &amp; in the HTML inside CDATA. Otherwise the XML will be malformed and osTicket will reject it.

5 Expected Output

When the flow runs successfully, the ticket appears in osTicket formatted like this:

Figure 8 — Example ticket output. Gray headers separate sections; left column shows labels, right column shows submitted values.
Click image to enlarge

6 Mapping Form Field IDs

Each form question has a unique response ID (e.g., r6c39cfe189244b05a9bae52d510d9601). To find them:

  1. Build the flow with the trigger, Get Response Details, and HTTP action as described above (you can use placeholder text in the Body initially).
  2. Save the flow and submit a test response to the form.
  3. Go to the flow’s Run History (click the flow name → see recent runs).
  4. Click on the successful run, then expand the “Get response details” action.
  5. In the Outputs section, you’ll see all the field IDs and their values in JSON format. Each key like "r6c39cfe1..." maps to a specific form question.
  6. Copy the r... IDs and replace the placeholders in your XML template.
Important

Field IDs are unique per form. You cannot reuse IDs from one form in another. Always extract IDs from a test run of the specific form you’re building for.

7 Current DSI Forms Reference

These forms are currently configured under the DSI service account. Each needs its own Power Automate flow:

Form Name Cluster Key? Notes
Cluster Account Request Yes — Sabine_ or Carya_ New HPC cluster account
Additional Storage Resources Yes — Sabine_ or Carya_ Storage expansion request
Additional Compute Resources Yes — Sabine_ or Carya_ Compute allocation request
New Project Allocation Request Yes — Sabine_ or Carya_ New project setup
Renew An Existing Project Yes — Sabine_ or Carya_ Project renewal
HPEDSI Workshop Support Request No Workshop/course support
HPEDSI Academic Support Request No Academic support ticket
Micro-Credential in Advanced Data Science No Badge verification application
Micro-Credential in High-Performance Computing No Badge verification application

8 Troubleshooting

Problem Cause & Fix
Ticket never appears in osTicket Check: (1) Is <cougarnet> populated? (2) For cluster forms, does the MS Form return exactly Sabine_ or Carya_? (3) Is the API key correct? Check the flow’s run history for HTTP response codes.
HTTP 400 / Bad Request XML is malformed. Common causes: unescaped & (use &amp;), missing closing tags, or broken CDATA section.
HTTP 401 / Unauthorized API key is wrong or expired. Verify the X-API-Key header value.
Ticket appears but fields are empty Response field IDs don’t match the form. Re-run a test submission, check “Get response details” output, and copy the correct r... IDs.
Cluster field rejected by osTicket The form’s cluster question must return exactly Sabine_ or Carya_ (with underscore). Check the form choice values — even a missing underscore will cause rejection.
Left column text wraps Increase the <td width="..."> on the first data row (try 280 or 300).
Form not in dropdown Form was created under a different account. Must be owned by the DSI service account.
Flow triggers but HTTP step is skipped Check if “Get response details” failed. Look at run history for the red X on the failed step.
Done

After completing these steps, every new form submission automatically generates a formatted ticket in osTicket. Always test with a sample submission before going live. Check the flow’s run history to verify the HTTP response returns 201 Created.
